What I got from reading about VanMoof's experience is that sometimes, simple solutions can solve big problems. Imagine this: the company was having trouble with bikes getting damaged during shipping, which was a huge headache because their bikes are fancy and expensive. But then, someone had a lightbulb moment: they started putting pictures of TVs on the shipping boxes. And guess what? It worked like magic! People started being extra careful with the boxes because they thought they were carrying TVs, not bikes. Isn't that clever?  This story teaches us a couple of things. First, it shows the power of thinking differently when faced with a problem. Sometimes, the solution isn't complicated; it's just about finding a new angle. Second, it tells us something about human nature. People tend to be more careful with valuable things, even if they're just pictures on a box. So, understanding what makes people tick can help us come up with smart solutions.  And you know what else? This story reminds us that setting clear goals can push us to find innovative solutions. VanMoof had a goal to sell lots of bikes online, and that pushed them to find a way to stop the shipping damage. So, it's all about being creative, understanding people, and keeping your eyes on the prize.

The delivery company will lose business if they are breaking items clearly labelled fragile during delivery at an exorbitant rate. While not really applying to me, who sends something via courier or post infrequently, the insight I gained is the clearing labelling something as fragile, or subtly (like the use of a TV graphic on the side), may result in the package being handled more carefully and thus being less likely to encounter damage in transit to the recipient.
